feynman.0: Wave a magnet near a coil of wire and — out of nothing but motion — a voltage appears. That’s the whole trick, and this page builds the three ideas hiding behind every power station in the world: magnetic flux, Faraday’s law for how big the induced emf is, and Lenz’s law for which way it goes.
feynman.1: It all starts with a magnetic field poking through a loop of wire. Magnetic flux ΦB is just our way of measuring how much of the field actually gets through the loop — think of it as counting the field lines that pierce the area the loop encloses. For a loop of area A:
feynman.2: That little subscript on B⊥ is reminding you about the geometry. It’s the slice of the field pointing perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that actually threads through the enclosed area, not the part lying flat in it. So the flux is biggest, ΦB = BA, when the field comes straight through the plane of the loop, and it drops to zero when the field skims along parallel to that plane, because then nothing pierces the loop at all. Tilt it somewhere in between and the flux sits somewhere in between too, as you can see in Figure 6.1; for VCE Physics you only ever need to say this in words — you’ll never have to crunch a number at an angle.
feynman.3: We measure flux in webers (Wb): 1 Wb = 1 T m². Take a 0.20 T field aimed straight through the plane of a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²) and you get ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. The one thing that’ll bite you: get the area into m² before you plug anything in. Convert the side lengths to metres first and then work out the area — remember 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m².
feynman.4: Key idea Flux is the field that gets through the area the loop encloses: ΦB = B⊥A, biggest when the field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, zero when the field lies parallel to that plane.
feynman.5: Since ΦB = B⊥A, there are only three things you can do to change the flux through a loop — count them. You can change the field strength B (bring a magnet closer or take it away, or turn the current in an electromagnet up and down); you can change the area A that’s sitting in the field (slide the loop into the field region or out of it); or you can change the orientation (spin the loop so that B⊥ changes). That third one can actually flip the flux right around through the loop, and it’s the trick every generator uses — which is exactly what the next page is about.
feynman.6: Back in 1831 Michael Faraday stumbled onto something marvellous: a changing magnetic flux through a coil conjures up an emf — a voltage ready to push a current around a closed circuit. It shows up the moment the flux changes, by any of those three methods, and it disappears the instant the flux stops changing. Faraday’s law tells you how big the induced emf is:
feynman.7: Here’s the key thing about Faraday’s law: it’s all about rate. The induced emf cares about how fast the flux changes — ΔΦB divided by Δt — and couldn’t care less about how much flux there is. A coil with a huge steady flux through it has zero emf; a coil whose tiny flux gets yanked away in a millisecond can have a whopping emf. Cut the time for the same flux change in half and you double the emf.
feynman.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there’s flux through the coil” — nope. Flux on its own induces nothing at all: a magnet sitting still inside a coil gives you loads of flux and exactly zero emf. It’s only the rate of change of flux that induces an emf, so every story you tell about induction has to name the change.
feynman.9: A coil with N turns acts like N loops wired one after another. The very same changing flux runs through every turn, and each turn’s emf adds onto the next, so doubling the turns doubles the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change in flux through one turn; the N out front does the multiplying.
feynman.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, with its plane perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. She pulls the coil completely out of the field in 0.10 s.
feynman.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), with ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb for each turn.
feynman.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — that’s the average emf while the coil is leaving the field. Do the same pull-out in 0.050 s and you’d get 8.0 V: same flux change, but twice as fast.
feynman.14: If you plot flux against time, the induced emf is just the gradient of that graph: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp means the emf holds steady; a steeper ramp means a bigger one; and a turning point — where the flux is momentarily sitting at a peak — means zero emf right at that instant. And it works backwards too: the area under an ε–t graph is NΔΦB, the whole flux change times the number of turns. Figure 6.2 shows the pair of graphs for a magnet dropping through a coil: the emf crosses zero at the exact moment the flux peaks, and the exit pulse comes out taller and narrower than the entry pulse, because the magnet’s picked up speed and leaves faster than it arrived — yet the two pulse areas come out equal.
feynman.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair — they’re never the same shape. The classic blunder is matching the look of one to the other, picking a flux graph shaped like the emf graph. Don’t. Read the gradient of the flux graph at each instant instead.
feynman.16: Connects A changing magnetic field making an electric field is one half of how an electromagnetic wave works. In Unit 4 you’ll see changing electric and magnetic fields keep re-creating each other and go sailing through empty space as light.
feynman.17: That minus sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field fights the change in flux that made it. The fight is always against the change, never against the field itself. While the flux through a loop is growing, the induced current’s field points opposite to the applied field, pushing back on the increase; while the flux is shrinking, the induced field points the same way as the applied field, trying to prop it up.
feynman.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” is wrong half the time. When the flux is shrinking, the induced current’s field points the same way as the applied field — it’s opposing the decrease. Always say which change is being opposed, never just the field on its own.
feynman.19: Lenz’s law isn’t some extra rule — it’s conservation of energy in disguise. Suppose the induced current went the other way and helped the change along: the growing flux would induce a bigger emf, which drives a bigger current, which grows the flux even more — and you’d be making electrical energy out of nothing. Opposition is the only way the books can balance, and it means work has to be done.
feynman.20: Push the north pole of a magnet toward a coil and the induced current turns the coil’s near face into a north pole too, so the magnet gets shoved back — and the work you do shoving against that repulsion is where the electrical energy comes from. It’s the same reason a generator gets harder to crank the more current it’s pumping out.
feynman.21: To find which way an induced current goes, follow three steps:
feynman.22: Try it on a north pole coming straight at a flat coil face: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet and is growing; the induced field has to point back toward the magnet to fight that growth; so with your thumb aimed at the magnet, your fingers show a current running anticlockwise as you look from the magnet’s side. Pull the magnet away instead and the flux shrinks — and every direction flips.
feynman.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect fights the change that made it. Any other direction would be making energy out of nothing, breaking conservation of energy.
feynman.24: Connects A generator is just AOS 2’s DC motor run backwards — the very same coil-in-a-field hardware. Feed current in and the F = nIlB forces spin the coil; spin the coil and it pushes current out, and Lenz’s law says the magnetic force on that induced current fights the rotation — so mechanical work in equals electrical energy out.

sagan.0: Move a magnet near a coil of wire and something quietly miraculous happens: out of motion alone, with no battery anywhere in sight, a voltage springs into being. This page builds the three ideas that lie behind every power station humming on this planet — magnetic flux, Faraday’s law for the size of the induced emf, and Lenz’s law for its direction.
sagan.1: It begins with a magnetic field threading its way through a loop of wire — the seed from which all electromagnetic induction grows. Magnetic flux ΦB measures how much of that field actually passes through the loop, and you can picture it simply as the number of field lines that pierce the area the loop encloses. For a loop of area A:
sagan.2: The little subscript on B⊥ is telling us about geometry. It is the component of the field standing perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that genuinely passes through the enclosed area, rather than skimming uselessly along within it. And so the flux reaches its greatest value, ΦB = BA, when the field stands square to the plane of the loop, and falls to nothing when the field lies flat in that plane, for then not a single line threads through. At the angles in between, the flux settles somewhere between these extremes, as shown in Figure 6.1; VCE Physics asks of you only this qualitative picture, never a calculation at an angle.
sagan.3: The unit of flux is the weber (Wb): 1 Wb = 1 T m². Imagine a 0.20 T field aimed perpendicular to the plane of a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²); the flux through it is ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. Areas must be carried in m² before you substitute — convert the side lengths to metres first and you will spare yourself grief, for 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m².
sagan.4: Key idea Flux is the field passing through the area enclosed by the loop: ΦB = B⊥A, greatest when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and vanishing when the field lies parallel to that plane.
sagan.5: Because ΦB = B⊥A, there are exactly three ways — and only three — to change the flux threading a loop: you may change the field strength B (carry a magnet nearer or further, or vary the current in an electromagnet); you may change the area A that sits within the field (slide the loop into or out of a field region); or you may change the orientation (rotate the loop so that B⊥ changes). This third path can turn the flux clean around, reversing its direction through the loop, and it is the very method at the heart of every generator humanity has ever built — the subject of the next page.
sagan.6: In 1831 Michael Faraday discovered something that would reshape the world: a changing magnetic flux through a coil calls forth an emf — a voltage able to drive a current around a closed circuit. The effect stirs to life whenever the flux changes, by any of the three methods above, and it dies away the very instant the flux holds still. Faraday’s law gives the size of the induced emf:
sagan.7: Faraday’s law is, at its heart, a law about rates. The induced emf cares only about how fast the flux changes — ΔΦB divided by Δt — and not at all about how large the flux happens to be. A coil bathed in a great steady flux produces no emf whatever; a coil whose modest flux is swept away in a millisecond can produce a mighty one. Halve the time taken for the same flux change and you double the emf.
sagan.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there is flux through the coil” — no. Flux alone induces nothing: a magnet sitting motionless inside a coil floods it with flux and yields exactly zero emf. Only the rate of change of flux can call an emf into being, and so every honest explanation of induction must name the change.
sagan.9: A coil of N turns behaves as N loops strung together in series. The very same changing flux threads through every turn alike, and the emf of each turn adds to the rest, so doubling the number of turns doubles the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change in flux through a single turn; the factor N does the multiplying.
sagan.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, its plane standing perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. In one swift motion she draws the coil completely out of the field in 0.10 s.
sagan.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), with ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb for each turn.
sagan.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — the average emf while the coil takes its leave of the field. Carry out that same withdrawal in 0.050 s and the result climbs to 8.0 V: an identical flux change, accomplished at twice the rate.
sagan.14: On a graph of flux against time, the induced emf reveals itself in the gradient: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp speaks of a constant emf; a steeper ramp, of a larger one; a turning point — the flux pausing momentarily at a peak — speaks of zero emf in that instant. And the relationship runs just as faithfully in reverse: the area beneath an ε–t graph equals NΔΦB, the total flux change multiplied by the number of turns. Figure 6.2 shows the pair of graphs for a magnet falling through a coil: the emf crosses zero at the very moment the flux reaches its summit, and the exit pulse stands taller and narrower than the entry pulse, because the accelerating magnet departs faster than it arrived — yet the two pulse areas are exactly equal.
sagan.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair, never twins in shape. To match the look of one graph to the other — to choose a flux graph shaped like the emf graph — is the classic blunder. Read the gradient of the flux graph at each instant instead.
sagan.16: Connects A changing magnetic field giving rise to an electric field is one half of the very mechanism by which an electromagnetic wave is born. In Unit 4 we shall see changing electric and magnetic fields endlessly regenerating one another, sweeping through the emptiness of a vacuum as light.
sagan.17: That negative sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law made visible: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field rises up to oppose the change in flux that gave it birth. The opposition is always to the change, never to the field itself. While the flux through a loop is swelling, the induced current’s field points against the applied field, resisting the increase; while the flux is fading, the induced field swings around to point the same way as the applied field, striving to sustain it.
sagan.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” is wrong half the time. When the flux is fading, the induced current’s field points in the same direction as the applied field — for it opposes the decrease. Always name the change being opposed, never the field alone.
sagan.19: Lenz’s law is no arbitrary rule; it is conservation of energy speaking through the magnets. For suppose the induced current reinforced the change instead: the growing flux would call forth a larger emf, which would drive a larger current, which would swell the flux further still — electrical energy pouring forth from nothing at all. Opposition is the only direction the universe permits, the only one in keeping with conservation of energy, and it demands that work be done.
sagan.20: Push the north pole of a magnet toward a coil and the induced current turns the near face of the coil into a north pole of its own, so the magnet is pushed back — and the work you do against that repulsion is the true source of the electrical energy. It is for this same reason that a generator grows harder to turn the more current it is asked to supply.
sagan.21: The direction of an induced current is found by a three-step method:
sagan.22: Apply it to a north pole approaching a flat coil face-on: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet and is increasing; the induced field must therefore point back toward the magnet to oppose that increase; and with the thumb aimed toward the magnet, the curling fingers reveal a current running anticlockwise as seen from the magnet. Draw the magnet away instead and the flux begins to fade, and every direction reverses.
sagan.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect opposes the very change that produces it. Any other direction would conjure energy out of nothing, in defiance of conservation of energy.
sagan.24: Connects A generator is nothing other than AOS 2’s DC motor run in reverse — the very same coil-in-a-field hardware. Feed current in and the F = nIlB forces set the coil spinning; spin the coil and current is pushed out, and by Lenz’s law the magnetic force on that induced current resists the rotation, so that mechanical work in equals electrical energy out.

cox.2: The little subscript on B⊥ is telling us about geometry. And so the flux is at its greatest, ΦB = BA, when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and falls to zero when the field lies parallel to that plane, because then no field lines pass through at all. cox.3: The unit of flux is the weber (Wb): 1 Wb = 1 T m². Take a 0.20 T field pointing perpendicular to the plane of a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²), and we get ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. The one thing to watch: areas must be in m² before you substitute. Convert the side lengths to metres first and the trouble disappears, because 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m².
cox.4: Key idea Flux is the field passing through the area enclosed by the loop: ΦB = B⊥A, greatest when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and zero when the field lies parallel to that plane.
cox.5: Because ΦB = B⊥A, there are only three ways to change the flux through a loop, and it is worth knowing all three. cox.6: In 1831 Michael Faraday discovered something rather wonderful: that a changing magnetic flux through a coil induces an emf — a voltage able to drive a current right around a closed circuit. The effect shows up whenever the flux changes, by any of the three methods above, and it vanishes the very instant the flux stops changing. Faraday’s law gives the size of the induced emf:
cox.7: Faraday’s law is, at heart, about rate. The induced emf depends on how fast the flux changes — ΔΦB divided by Δt — and not at all on how large the flux happens to be. A coil threaded by an enormous steady flux has no emf whatsoever; a coil whose small flux is whisked away in a millisecond can have a large one. cox.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there is flux through the coil” — no. Flux on its own induces nothing at all: a magnet sitting quietly inside a coil gives you a large flux and exactly zero emf. cox.9: A coil of N turns behaves like N loops in series. The same changing flux threads through every turn, and the emf from each turn adds to the next, so doubling the number of turns doubles the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change in flux through one turn; it is the factor N that does the multiplying.
cox.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, with its plane perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. She pulls the coil completely out of the field in 0.10 s.

cox.16: Connects A changing magnetic field giving rise to an electric field is one half of the mechanism behind an electromagnetic wave. In Unit 4 we shall see changing electric and magnetic fields regenerate one another and travel through empty space as light.

The opposition is always to the change, never to the field itself. munroe.0: Hold a magnet near a coil of wire and move it, and a voltage appears. You didn’t plug anything in. The motion did it. This page assembles the three ideas that sit underneath every power station on Earth: magnetic flux, Faraday’s law for how big the induced emf is, and Lenz’s law for which way it points.
munroe.1: Everything starts with a magnetic field going through a loop of wire. Magnetic flux ΦB is a measure of how much magnetic field passes through the loop — count the field lines that go through the area the loop encloses, and that count is the flux. For a loop of area A:
munroe.2: The little subscript on B⊥ is doing real work. It means the part of the field that points perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that actually goes through the area, not the part lying flat across it. So the flux is biggest, ΦB = BA, when the field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and it’s zero when the field lies parallel to that plane, because in that case nothing goes through the loop at all. In between, the flux is somewhere in between, as Figure 6.1 shows. VCE Physics only ever asks you to say that in words; it never asks you to calculate the flux at an angle.
munroe.3: The unit of flux is the weber (Wb), and 1 Wb = 1 T m². Take a 0.20 T field pointing straight through a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²) and you get ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. Put the area in m² before you substitute, not after. Convert the side lengths to metres first and then work out the area — that order avoids the mistake — because 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m².
munroe.4: Key idea Flux is the field that goes through the area the loop encloses: ΦB = B⊥A, biggest when the field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, zero when the field lies parallel to that plane.
munroe.5: Since ΦB = B⊥A, there are exactly three things you can change to change the flux through a loop, and that’s it. You can change the field strength B (move a magnet closer or further away, or turn the current in an electromagnet up or down); you can change the area A that sits inside the field (slide the loop into or out of the field region); or you can change the orientation (rotate the loop so that B⊥ changes). That third one can flip the flux around to point the other way through the loop, and it’s the trick every generator uses, which is what the next page is about.
munroe.6: In 1831 Michael Faraday found that when the magnetic flux through a coil changes, the coil gets an emf — a voltage that can push a current around a closed circuit. It shows up whenever the flux changes, by any of the three methods above, and it disappears the instant the flux stops changing. Not slowly. The instant. Faraday’s law tells you how big the induced emf is:
munroe.7: Faraday’s law is about a rate, not an amount. The induced emf depends on how fast the flux changes — that’s ΔΦB divided by Δt — and it does not care one bit how much flux there is. A coil with an enormous steady flux through it has zero emf. A coil with a tiny flux that you yank away in a millisecond can have a large one. Cut the time for the same flux change in half and you double the emf.
munroe.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there’s flux through the coil” — no. Flux by itself induces nothing. A magnet sitting still inside a coil produces plenty of flux and exactly zero emf. It’s only the rate of change of flux that induces anything, so every explanation of induction has to say what’s changing.
munroe.9: A coil of N turns acts like N separate loops wired in series. The same changing flux goes through every turn, and each turn’s emf adds to the next, so doubling the number of turns doubles the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change in flux through one turn; the N out front is what multiplies it up.
munroe.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, with its plane perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. She pulls the coil all the way out of the field in 0.10 s.
munroe.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), where ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb for each turn.
munroe.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — the average emf for as long as the coil is leaving the field. Do the exact same pull in 0.050 s instead and you get 8.0 V: same flux change, twice as fast.
munroe.14: On a graph of flux against time, you read the induced emf off the gradient: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp means a constant emf; a steeper ramp means a bigger one; a turning point — where the flux is momentarily at a maximum — means the emf is zero at that exact instant. It works backwards too: the area under an ε–t graph equals NΔΦB, the total flux change times the number of turns. Figure 6.2 shows both graphs for a magnet falling through a coil: the emf crosses zero at the very moment the flux peaks, and the exit pulse is taller and narrower than the entry pulse, because the magnet has been accelerating the whole way down and so leaves faster than it arrived — but the two pulse areas come out equal.
munroe.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair; they are never the same shape. Grabbing a flux graph that looks like the emf graph — picking the one whose shape matches — is the classic mistake. Read the gradient of the flux graph at each instant instead.
munroe.16: Connects A changing magnetic field producing an electric field is one half of how an electromagnetic wave works. In Unit 4, changing electric and magnetic fields keep regenerating each other and travel through a vacuum as light.
munroe.17: The minus sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field opposes the change in flux that made it. It opposes the change, not the field itself — this is the part people get wrong. While the flux through a loop is going up, the induced current’s field points opposite to the applied field, fighting the increase; while the flux is going down, the induced field points the same way as the applied field, propping it up.
munroe.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” is wrong half the time. When the flux is decreasing, the induced current’s field points the same way as the applied field — it’s opposing the decrease, not the field. Always say which change is being opposed, never just the field.
munroe.19: Lenz’s law is just conservation of energy in disguise. Suppose it went the other way and the induced current helped the change along: then the growing flux would induce a bigger emf, which would drive a bigger current, which would grow the flux more, and so on — electrical energy out of nothing, no input required. That can’t happen, so opposition is the only option left, and it means work has to be done.
munroe.20: Push the north pole of a magnet toward a coil and the induced current turns the near face of the coil into a north pole too, so the magnet gets pushed back, and the work you do shoving against that push is where the electrical energy comes from. It’s the same reason a generator gets harder to turn the more current it’s supplying.
munroe.21: To work out which way an induced current goes, run the same three steps every time:
munroe.22: Take a north pole coming straight at a flat coil face: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet and is increasing; so the induced field has to point back toward the magnet to fight that increase; point your thumb toward the magnet and your fingers curl the way the current goes — anticlockwise, seen from the magnet. Pull the magnet away instead and the flux is now decreasing, and every direction flips.
munroe.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect opposes the change that’s producing it. Any other direction would make energy out of nothing, which conservation of energy doesn’t allow.
munroe.24: Connects A generator is just AOS 2’s DC motor run backwards — the very same coil-in-a-field hardware. Feed current in and the F = nIlB forces spin the coil; spin the coil and it pushes current out, and by Lenz’s law the magnetic force on that induced current fights the rotation, so the mechanical work you put in equals the electrical energy you get out.

noether.9: A coil of N turns behaves as N loops in series. One and the same changing flux threads every turn, the per-turn emfs add, and so doubling the number of turns doubles the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change in flux through one turn; the factor N performs the multiplying.

noether.16: Connects A changing magnetic field giving rise to an electric field is one half of the mechanism of an electromagnetic wave. In Unit 4, changing electric and magnetic fields regenerate one another and travel through a vacuum as light.
noether.17: The negative sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field opposes the change in flux that produced it. What is opposed is always the change, never the field itself. While the flux through a loop increases, the induced current’s field points opposite to the applied field, opposing the increase; while the flux decreases, the induced field points the same way as the applied field, sustaining it. mack.2: That little subscript on B⊥ is doing geometry for you. It’s the slice of the field pointing perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that actually punches through the enclosed area, not the part lying flat in the plane doing nothing. So the flux is at its biggest, ΦB = BA, when the field stabs straight through perpendicular to the loop’s plane, and drops to zero when the field skims parallel to that plane, because then not a single line makes it through. mack.5: Look at ΦB = B⊥A and it hands you exactly three levers for changing the flux through a loop — no more, no fewer. veritasium.3: The unit of flux is the weber (Wb): 1 Wb = 1 T m². Take a 0.20 T field aimed straight through a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²) and you get ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. Now here’s where people quietly lose marks: the area has to be in m² before you substitute. Convert the side lengths to metres first, then find the area, because 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m² — and that gap is exactly the size of a wrong answer.
veritasium.4: Key idea Flux is the field passing through the area enclosed by the loop: ΦB = B⊥A, maximum when the field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, zero when the field is parallel to that plane.
veritasium.5: Now look at ΦB = B⊥A and ask: what could possibly make that number change? There are exactly three answers, no more. You can change the field strength B (bring a magnet closer or further, or dial the current in an electromagnet up and down); you can change the area A sitting inside the field (slide the loop in or out of the field region); or you can change the orientation (rotate the loop so that B⊥ shifts). That third one is the sneaky powerful one — it can actually flip the direction of the flux through the loop, and it’s the trick running inside every generator ever built, which is exactly where the next page goes.
veritasium.6: In 1831 Michael Faraday found something that should still feel a little magical: a changing magnetic flux through a coil conjures up an emf — a voltage ready to push a current around a closed circuit. It shows up the moment the flux changes, by any of the three methods above, and it disappears the instant the flux stops changing. Faraday’s law tells you how big that induced emf is:
veritasium.7: Here’s the part people get backwards. Faraday’s law is about rate, not amount. The induced emf depends on how fast the flux changes — ΔΦB divided by Δt — and not at all on how big the flux happens to be. A coil drowning in a huge but steady flux produces zero emf. A coil with a tiny flux that vanishes in a millisecond can produce a big one. Cut the time for the same flux change in half, and you double the emf.
veritasium.8: Trap You’ll be tempted to write “an emf is induced because there’s flux through the coil.” No. Flux on its own induces precisely nothing: park a magnet motionless inside a coil and you get a large flux and exactly zero emf. It’s only the rate of change of flux that induces an emf, so every explanation you give has to name the change.
veritasium.9: What about a coil with N turns? Picture it as N separate loops wired in series. The very same changing flux threads through every single turn, and each turn’s emf adds onto the last, so double the turns and you double the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the flux change through just one turn; the factor N does the multiplying for you.
veritasium.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, with its plane perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. She pulls the coil completely out of the field in 0.10 s.
veritasium.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), with ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb for each turn.
veritasium.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — the average emf for as long as the coil is leaving the field. Now watch what rate does: pull the coil out in 0.050 s instead and you’d get 8.0 V. Same flux change, twice the speed, double the voltage.
veritasium.14: Here’s a shift that trips a lot of people up. On a graph of flux against time, the induced emf isn’t the height of the curve — it’s the gradient: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp gives you a constant emf; a steeper ramp gives a bigger one; and a turning point — the flux momentarily sitting at a peak — gives zero emf at that instant, even though the flux is at its largest. The relationship even runs in reverse: the area under an ε–t graph equals NΔΦB, the total flux change times the number of turns. Figure 6.2 shows the pair of graphs for a magnet dropping through a coil: the emf crosses zero at the exact moment the flux peaks, and the exit pulse is taller and narrower than the entry pulse because the accelerating magnet leaves faster than it arrived — yet the two pulse areas come out equal.
veritasium.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair — they will never be the same shape. The classic mistake is to glance at one and draw the other to match its look, picking a flux graph shaped like the emf graph. Don’t copy the shape. Read the gradient of the flux graph at each instant instead.
veritasium.16: Connects A changing magnetic field giving birth to an electric field is one half of the machinery of an electromagnetic wave. In Unit 4 you’ll see changing electric and magnetic fields regenerate each other and race through empty space as light.
veritasium.17: So what is that negative sign in Faraday’s law actually doing? It’s Lenz’s law: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field opposes the change in flux that created it. And read that carefully — it opposes the change, never the field itself. While the flux through a loop is growing, the induced current’s field points against the applied field, fighting the increase; while the flux is shrinking, the induced field flips and points the same way as the applied field, trying to prop it up.
veritasium.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” sounds right, and it’s wrong exactly half the time. When the flux is decreasing, the induced current’s field points the same direction as the applied field — because now it’s opposing the decrease. Always say which change is being opposed, never the field on its own.
veritasium.19: Why does nature insist on opposition? Conservation of energy demands it. Imagine the opposite for a second: suppose the induced current reinforced the change. Then the growing flux would induce a bigger emf, driving a bigger current, growing the flux still further — a runaway loop spitting out electrical energy from nothing at all. That can’t happen. Opposition is the only direction the universe allows, and it’s the direction that forces you to do work to get your energy.
veritasium.20: Push the north pole of a magnet toward a coil and the induced current turns the coil’s near face into a north pole too — so it shoves back, repelling the magnet. The work you do fighting that repulsion is precisely where the electrical energy comes from. There’s no free lunch. It’s the same reason a generator gets stiffer and harder to turn the more current you draw from it.
veritasium.21: To find which way an induced current actually flows, use a three-step method:
veritasium.22: Try it on a north pole coming straight at a flat coil face-on: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet and is increasing; so the induced field has to point back toward the magnet to fight that increase; now point your thumb toward the magnet and your fingers curl out the current — anticlockwise as seen from the magnet’s side. Pull the magnet away instead and the flux now decreases, and every single direction flips.
veritasium.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect opposes the change producing it. Any other direction would produce energy without an energy input, violating conservation of energy.
veritasium.24: Connects A generator is just AOS 2’s DC motor run backwards — the very same coil-in-a-field hardware. Feed current in and the F = nIlB forces spin the coil; spin the coil and it pushes current out, and by Lenz’s law the magnetic force on that induced current fights the rotation — so the mechanical work you put in equals the electrical energy you get out.

attenborough.0: Move a magnet near a coil of wire, and from motion alone — from nothing but the gesture itself — a voltage quietly appears. On this page we shall watch three ideas take shape, the three that lie behind every power station on Earth: magnetic flux, Faraday’s law for the size of the induced emf, and Lenz’s law for the direction it chooses.
attenborough.1: It begins, always, with a magnetic field passing through a loop of wire. Magnetic flux ΦB is our measure of how much of that field threads its way through the loop — the number of field lines that pass through the area the loop encloses. For a loop of area A:
attenborough.2: That little subscript on B⊥ is telling us something about geometry. It is the component of the field that stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that truly passes through the enclosed area, rather than lying flat within it. And so the flux reaches its greatest value, ΦB = BA, when the field strikes perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and falls to nothing when the field lies parallel to that plane, for then not a single line passes through. At the angles in between, the flux rests somewhere between these two extremes, as shown in Figure 6.1. VCE Physics asks only for this qualitative picture — never for a calculation at an angle.
attenborough.3: The unit of flux is the weber (Wb): 1 Wb = 1 T m². Consider a 0.20 T field directed perpendicular to the plane of a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²); it yields ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. The areas must be in m² before you substitute. Convert the side lengths to metres first, and the trap is avoided — for 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m².
attenborough.4: Key idea Flux is the field passing through the area the loop encloses: ΦB = B⊥A, greatest when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and nothing at all when the field lies parallel to that plane.
attenborough.5: Because ΦB = B⊥A, there are exactly three ways — no more — to change the flux through a loop. You may change the field strength B (carry a magnet nearer or further, or vary the current in an electromagnet); you may change the area A that sits within the field (slide the loop into the field region, or out of it); or you may change the orientation (rotate the loop, so that B⊥ itself shifts). This third way can turn the flux clean around, reversing its direction through the loop — and it is the way of every generator, which is the subject of the next page.
attenborough.6: In 1831 Michael Faraday made his great discovery: that a changing magnetic flux through a coil induces an emf — a voltage able to drive a current around a closed circuit. The effect stirs into life whenever the flux changes, by any of the three ways above, and it vanishes the very instant the flux holds still. Faraday’s law gives us the size of that induced emf:
attenborough.7: Faraday’s law, you see, is a law of rates. The induced emf depends on how fast the flux changes — ΔΦB divided by Δt — and not at all on how large the flux happens to be. A coil threaded by a great steady flux has no emf whatsoever; a coil whose small flux is swept away in a single millisecond can have a large one. Halve the time taken for the same change of flux, and the emf doubles.
attenborough.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there is flux through the coil” — no. Flux alone induces nothing at all: a magnet resting motionless inside a coil holds a great flux and produces exactly zero emf. Only the rate of change of flux can induce an emf, and so every explanation of induction must name the change.
attenborough.9: A coil of N turns behaves as N loops set in series. The same changing flux threads through every turn, and the emfs of each turn add together — so doubling the number of turns doubles the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change in flux through one turn; it is the factor N that does the multiplying.
attenborough.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, its plane perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. In 0.10 s she pulls the coil completely clear of the field.
attenborough.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), with ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb for each turn.
attenborough.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — the average emf as the coil departs the field. Had the same withdrawal been completed in 0.050 s, it would give 8.0 V: an identical change of flux, accomplished at twice the rate.
attenborough.14: On a graph of flux against time, the induced emf reveals itself in the gradient: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp means a steady emf; a steeper ramp, a larger one; and a turning point — the flux pausing for an instant at its maximum — means zero emf in that very moment. The relationship runs the other way, too: the area beneath an ε–t graph equals NΔΦB, the whole change of flux multiplied by the number of turns. Figure 6.2 shows the pair of graphs for a magnet falling through a coil: the emf crosses zero at the very instant the flux peaks, and the exit pulse stands taller and narrower than the entry pulse, for the magnet, now accelerating, leaves faster than it arrived — yet the two pulse areas are equal.
attenborough.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair, and never the same shape. To match the look of one to the other — to choose a flux graph shaped like the emf graph — is the classic mistake. Read the gradient of the flux graph at each instant instead.
attenborough.16: Connects A changing magnetic field giving rise to an electric field is one half of the very mechanism of an electromagnetic wave. In Unit 4, changing electric and magnetic fields will regenerate each other, again and again, and travel through the emptiness of a vacuum as light.
attenborough.17: The negative sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field opposes the change in flux that gave it birth. The opposition is always to the change, never to the field itself. While the flux through a loop is rising, the induced current’s field points against the applied field, resisting the increase; while the flux is falling, the induced field turns the same way as the applied field, striving to sustain it.
attenborough.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” is wrong half the time. When the flux is falling, the induced current’s field points in the same direction as the applied field — for it opposes the decrease. Always name the change being opposed, never the field alone.
attenborough.19: Lenz’s law is, at heart, a consequence of the conservation of energy. Imagine the induced current did the opposite — that it reinforced the change. The growing flux would induce a larger emf, driving a larger current, swelling the flux further still, and electrical energy would pour forth from nothing, with no energy fed in. Opposition is the only direction that nature permits, the only one consistent with conservation of energy — and it demands that work be done.
attenborough.20: Push the north pole of a magnet toward a coil, and it induces a current that turns the near face of the coil into a north pole of its own — so the magnet is repelled, and the work you do against that repulsion is the very source of the electrical energy. It is the same opposition that makes a generator grow harder to turn the more current it supplies.
attenborough.21: The direction of an induced current is found by a three-step method:
attenborough.22: Apply it to a north pole approaching a flat coil, face-on: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet, and it is increasing; the induced field must therefore point back toward the magnet, to oppose that increase; and with the thumb pointing toward the magnet, the fingers trace a current running anticlockwise, as seen from the magnet. Pull the magnet away instead, and the flux begins to fall — and every direction reverses.
attenborough.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect opposes the change that produces it. Any other direction would conjure energy from nothing, violating the conservation of energy.
attenborough.24: Connects A generator is AOS 2’s DC motor run in reverse — the very same coil-in-a-field hardware. Feed current in, and the F = nIlB forces spin the coil; spin the coil, and it pushes current out — and by Lenz’s law the magnetic force on that induced current resists the rotation, so the mechanical work going in equals the electrical energy coming out.

greekepic.0: Bear a magnet near a coil of wire, and from motion alone a voltage is born. This song raises the three pillars upon which every power-house of the world is founded: magnetic flux, the law of Faraday for the measure of the induced emf, and the law of Lenz for the way it turns.
greekepic.1: A magnetic field that passes through a loop of wire — here is the wellspring of electromagnetic induction. Magnetic flux ΦB reckons how much of the field threads the loop, even as one might count the field lines that pass through the area the loop encloses. For a loop of area A:
greekepic.2: The mark upon B⊥ declares the geometry of the thing. It is that part of the field standing perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that pierces the enclosed area rather than lying flat within it. So the flux swells to its utmost, ΦB = BA, when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and falls to nothing when the field lies parallel to that plane, for then no line passes through the loop at all. At the angles between, the flux dwells between these two bounds, as is shown in Figure 6.1; yet VCE Physics asks only this telling in words, and never a reckoning at an angle.
greekepic.3: The unit of flux is the weber (Wb): 1 Wb = 1 T m². A 0.20 T field aimed perpendicular to the plane of a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²) yields ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. Let the areas be cast in m² before ever you substitute. Turn the side-lengths to metres before reckoning the area, and you shall not stumble, for 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², and not 10⁻² m².
greekepic.4: Key idea Flux is the field that passes through the area the loop encloses: ΦB = B⊥A, greatest when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and nothing when the field lies parallel to that plane.
greekepic.5: Because ΦB = B⊥A, there are but three roads by which the flux through a loop may be altered: alter the strength of the field B (bear a magnet nearer or bear it away, or stir the current within an electromagnet); alter the area A that lies within the field (slide the loop into the field's domain or out of it); or alter the orientation (turn the loop so that B⊥ is changed). This third road may reverse the very direction of the flux through the loop, and it is the road trodden by every generator — the matter of the next page.
greekepic.6: In the year 1831 Michael Faraday beheld that a changing magnetic flux through a coil calls forth an emf — a voltage with the power to drive a current round a closed circuit. This wonder appears whensoever the flux changes, by any of the three roads above, and it perishes in the very instant the flux ceases to change. Faraday’s law gives the measure of the induced emf:
greekepic.7: Faraday’s law is a law of rate. The induced emf hangs upon how swiftly the flux changes — ΔΦB divided by Δt — and cares nothing for how great the flux may be. A coil threaded by a vast and steady flux bears no emf at all; a coil whose meagre flux is stripped away in a single millisecond may bear a mighty one. Halve the time given to the same flux change, and you double the emf.
greekepic.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there is flux through the coil” — nay. Flux alone induces nothing: a magnet that rests unmoving within a coil pours forth a great flux and an emf of exactly zero. Only the rate of change of flux induces an emf, and so every telling of induction must name the change.
greekepic.9: A coil of N turns bears itself as N loops set in series. The selfsame changing flux threads every turn, and the emfs of each turn are joined together, so that to double the count of turns is to double the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the change of flux through one turn; the factor N performs the multiplying.
greekepic.10: Shanna holds a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, its plane standing perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. In 0.10 s she draws the coil wholly out of the field.
greekepic.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), with ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb for each turn.
greekepic.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — the average emf while the coil departs the field. The same withdrawal accomplished in 0.050 s would yield 8.0 V: the selfsame flux change wrought at twice the speed.
greekepic.14: Upon a graph of flux against time, the induced emf is read from the gradient: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp speaks of a constant emf; a steeper ramp, of a greater one; a turning point — the flux paused for an instant at its height — speaks of zero emf in that very moment. And the bond runs likewise backward: the area beneath an ε–t graph equals NΔΦB, the whole change of flux multiplied by the count of turns. Figure 6.2 shows the twin graphs for a magnet falling through a coil: the emf crosses zero at the very moment the flux attains its peak, and the pulse of departure stands taller and narrower than the pulse of entry, for the quickening magnet leaves faster than it came, while the areas of the two pulses are equal.
greekepic.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair, and never of one shape. To match the look of the one to the other — to choose a flux graph fashioned like the emf graph — is the time-worn error. Read instead the gradient of the flux graph at each instant.
greekepic.16: Connects A changing magnetic field that begets an electric field is one half of the engine of an electromagnetic wave. In Unit 4, changing electric and magnetic fields beget one another anew, and journey through the void as light.
greekepic.17: The negative sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field opposes the change in flux that gave it birth. The opposition is to the change, and never to the field itself. While the flux through a loop is swelling, the field of the induced current points against the applied field, opposing the rise; while the flux is dwindling, the induced field points the same way as the applied field, upholding it.
greekepic.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” speaks falsely the half of the time. When the flux is dwindling, the field of the induced current points the same way as the applied field — it opposes the fall. Always name the change that is opposed, and never the field alone.
greekepic.19: Lenz’s law flows from the conservation of energy. Were the induced current instead to reinforce the change, the swelling flux would induce a greater emf, driving a greater current and swelling the flux yet further, so that electrical energy would spring forth with no energy poured in. Opposition is the one direction that keeps faith with the conservation of energy, and it demands that work be done.
greekepic.20: Thrust the north pole of a magnet toward a coil, and there is induced a current that makes of the coil’s near face a north pole, so that the magnet is driven back, and the work done against that driving-back is the very fountain of the electrical energy. By this same opposition does a generator grow harder to turn the more current it pours out.
greekepic.21: The direction of an induced current is found by a method of three steps:
greekepic.22: Set it upon a north pole drawing near a flat coil, face to face: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet and is swelling; the induced field must point back toward the magnet to oppose the rise; with the thumb toward the magnet, the fingers reveal a current that runs anticlockwise as the magnet beholds it. Draw the magnet away instead, and the flux dwindles, and every direction is reversed.
greekepic.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect opposes the change that bore it. Any other direction would beget energy with no energy poured in, breaking the conservation of energy.
greekepic.24: Connects A generator is AOS 2’s DC motor run backward — the selfsame hardware of a coil within a field. Feed current in, and F = nIlB forces spin the coil; spin the coil, and current is pushed out, and by Lenz’s law the magnetic force upon that induced current withstands the turning, so that mechanical work in equals electrical energy out.

cnpop.0: 注意看，把一块磁铁往线圈旁边一凑，啥都不接，电压就这么凭空冒出来了——光靠运动就能发电。这一页我们要搭起每座发电站背后的三块基石：磁通量、管感应电动势大小的法拉第定律，还有管它方向的楞次定律。
cnpop.1: 一切的起点，是一个磁场穿过一圈导线。磁通量 ΦB 衡量的就是有多少磁场穿过了这个线圈，说白了，就是有多少根磁感线钻过了线圈围出来的那块面积。对于一个面积为 A 的线圈：
cnpop.2: B⊥ 上那个小角标，讲的是几何关系。它指的是磁场里垂直于线圈平面的那个分量——也就是真正穿过线圈面积、而不是躺在平面里划水的那部分。所以当磁场垂直于线圈平面时，磁通量拉满，ΦB = BA；当磁场平躺在这个平面里时，磁通量直接归零，因为这时候没有一根磁感线能钻过线圈。介于两者之间的角度，磁通量就卡在这俩值中间，如图 6.1 所示；VCE 物理只要你会定性地说清楚这一点，永远不会让你算斜着角度的具体数值，放心。
cnpop.3: 磁通量的单位是韦伯（Wb）：1 Wb = 1 T m²。来个例子：一个 0.20 T 的磁场垂直穿过一个 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm 的线圈（A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²），算出来 ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb。划重点：代入前面积必须先换成 m²。先把边长换成米再去算面积，能帮你躲掉一个大坑，因为 1 cm² 是 10⁻⁴ m²，可不是 10⁻² m²，别搞混了。
cnpop.4: Key idea 磁通量就是穿过线圈所围面积的那部分磁场：ΦB = B⊥A，磁场垂直于线圈平面时拉满，平行于该平面时归零。
cnpop.5: 因为 ΦB = B⊥A，所以想改变穿过线圈的磁通量，统共就三条路，一条都不多：改磁场强度 B（把磁铁挪近挪远，或者调电磁铁里的电流）；改处在磁场里的面积 A（把线圈滑进或滑出磁场区域）；或者改朝向（转动线圈，让 B⊥ 跟着变）。第三招最猛，它能把穿过线圈的磁通量方向直接掀个底朝天——每一台发电机用的都是这一招，这正是下一页要讲的主角。
cnpop.6: 1831 年，迈克尔·法拉第发现了一件大事：变化的磁通量穿过线圈，会感应出一个电动势——一个能在闭合电路里驱动电流跑起来的电压。只要磁通量在变，不管用上面哪一招，这效应就会现身；而磁通量一旦停止变化，它当场原地消失。法拉第定律给出了感应电动势的大小：
cnpop.7: 法拉第定律本质上是个变化率的关系。感应电动势取决于磁通量变得有多快——ΔΦB 除以 Δt——而跟磁通量本身有多大半毛钱关系都没有。一个被巨大恒定磁通量穿过的线圈，电动势是零；而一个本来磁通量很小、却在一毫秒内被抽走的线圈，电动势能大得吓人。同样的磁通量变化，时间砍一半，电动势直接翻倍。
cnpop.8: Trap “因为线圈里有磁通量，所以感应出了电动势”——不，错得离谱。光有磁通量啥都感应不出来：一块磁铁稳稳停在线圈里，磁通量大得很，电动势却精准地等于零。只有磁通量的变化率才能感应出电动势，所以你对感应现象的每一句解释，都必须点名那个“变化”。
cnpop.9: 一个 N 匝的线圈，表现得就像 N 个串联起来的小线圈。同一个变化的磁通量穿过每一匝，每一匝的电动势叠加起来，所以匝数翻倍，电动势就翻倍。公式里的 ΔΦB 是穿过一匝的磁通量变化；负责做乘法的，是 N 这个因子。
cnpop.10: Shanna 手里拿着一个 200 匝的矩形线圈，5.0 cm × 8.0 cm，线圈平面垂直于一个 0.50 T 的匀强磁场。她在 0.10 s 内把线圈整个抽出了磁场。
cnpop.11: 公式：ε = N ΔΦBΔt（取大小），其中每一匝的 ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb。
cnpop.13: 答案：ε = 4.0 V——这是线圈离开磁场期间的平均电动势。同样的抽取动作要是在 0.050 s 内完成，就能飙到 8.0 V：一模一样的磁通量变化，只是速率快了一倍。
cnpop.14: 在磁通量对时间的图上，感应电动势就从斜率里读出来：ε = −N × ΦB–t 图的斜率。一段笔直的斜坡，意味着恒定的电动势；坡越陡，电动势越大；而在拐点处——磁通量瞬间达到最大值——那一刻的电动势是零。这关系还能倒着玩：ε–t 图下的面积等于 NΔΦB，也就是总磁通量变化乘以匝数。图 6.2 给出了一块磁铁穿过线圈下落时的那对图：电动势恰好在磁通量冲顶的那一瞬间穿过零点，而且出来的那个脉冲又高又窄，比进去时更尖，因为加速下落的磁铁离开时比进来时更快——但两个脉冲的面积是相等的。
cnpop.15: Trap ΦB–t 图和它的 ε–t 图是一对“斜率搭档”，形状永远不会一样。把一个图的长相硬套到另一个上——挑一个长得像电动势图的磁通量图——这是教科书级的经典翻车。正确操作是：在每个瞬间去读磁通量图的斜率。
cnpop.16: Connects 变化的磁场会产生电场，这正是电磁波运行机制的一半。在第四单元里，变化的电场和磁场会互相再生，手拉手以光的形式穿越真空狂奔。
cnpop.17: 法拉第定律里那个负号，就是楞次定律：感应电动势驱动的电流，它自己产生的磁场，会反抗那个把它生出来的磁通量变化。它反抗的永远是“变化”，绝不是磁场本身。当穿过线圈的磁通量正在增大时，感应电流的磁场指向跟外加磁场相反，对着增大死命顶；当磁通量正在减小时，感应磁场又调头指向跟外加磁场相同的方向，想方设法把它续住。
cnpop.18: Trap “感应磁场反抗外加磁场”这句话，有一半时候是错的。当磁通量正在减小时，感应电流的磁场指向跟外加磁场相同的方向——它反抗的是“减小”。永远要说清楚被反抗的那个“变化”，别光秃秃地甩一个“磁场”。
cnpop.19: 楞次定律说到底，是能量守恒的必然结果。反过来想：要是感应电流不去反抗、反而去助推这个变化，那增大的磁通量就会感应出更大的电动势，驱动更大的电流，把磁通量推得更大……于是不用喂一点能量进去，电能就源源不断地凭空冒出来了。只有“反抗”这一个方向跟能量守恒不冲突，而且它要求必须有人做功。
cnpop.20: 把磁铁的 N 极往线圈推过去，感应出的电流会把线圈靠近磁铁那一面变成一个 N 极，于是磁铁被弹开；而你顶着这股排斥力做的功，正是那份电能的来源。同样的道理也解释了：为什么发电机供出的电流越大，它就转得越费劲。
cnpop.21: 判断感应电流的方向，靠一套三步法：
cnpop.22: 拿一个 N 极正对着线圈平面冲过去来试：穿过线圈的磁通量指向背离磁铁的方向，并且正在增大；为了反抗这个增大，感应磁场必须掉头指回磁铁；大拇指指向磁铁，四指弯过去显示出来的电流方向，从磁铁这头看过去就是逆时针。要是反过来把磁铁抽走，磁通量改为减小，那每一个方向就全部反转。
cnpop.23: Key idea 感应电动势驱动的电流，其效果总是去反抗那个产生它的变化。换成任何别的方向，都等于不喂能量就凭空造能量，妥妥违反能量守恒。
cnpop.24: Connects 发电机就是 AOS 2 里那台直流电动机倒着开——同一套“线圈泡在磁场里”的硬件。喂电流进去，F = nIlB 的力就推着线圈转；转动线圈，电流就被推出来，而根据楞次定律，作用在这股感应电流上的磁力会反抗转动，所以喂进去的机械功，正好等于产出的电能。

ai.0: Wave a magnet near a coil of wire and a voltage springs up out of nothing but motion — no battery, no plug, just movement. This page builds the three ideas that quietly power every power station on Earth: magnetic flux, Faraday’s law for the size of the induced emf, and Lenz’s law for its direction.
ai.1: It all begins with a magnetic field threading its way through a loop of wire. Magnetic flux ΦB is your measure of how much field gets through that loop — picture it as the number of field lines spearing through the area the loop encloses. For a loop of area A:
ai.2: That little subscript on B⊥ is telling you about geometry. It’s the slice of the field pointing perpendicular to the plane of the loop — the part that actually punches through the enclosed area, rather than skimming along inside it. So the flux hits its maximum, ΦB = BA, when the field stands perpendicular to the plane of the loop, and drops to zero when the field lies flat in that plane, because then not a single field line gets through. Tilt it somewhere in between and the flux sits between those two extremes, as you can see in Figure 6.1. Reassuringly, VCE Physics only ever asks you for this qualitative picture — never a calculation at an angle.
ai.3: Flux is measured in webers (Wb), where 1 Wb = 1 T m². Suppose a 0.20 T field points straight through the plane of a 5.0 cm × 5.0 cm loop (A = 0.050 × 0.050 = 2.5 × 10⁻³ m²). The flux comes out as ΦB = 0.20 × 2.5 × 10⁻³ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ Wb. The golden rule: get your areas into m² before you substitute. Convert the side lengths to metres first and then work out the area — it saves you the classic slip, because 1 cm² is 10⁻⁴ m², not 10⁻² m².
ai.4: Key idea Flux is the field getting through the area the loop encloses: ΦB = B⊥A — greatest when the field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop, zero when the field lies parallel to that plane.
ai.5: Since ΦB = B⊥A, there are exactly three levers you can pull to change the flux through a loop. You can change the field strength B — bring a magnet closer or pull it back, or dial the current in an electromagnet up and down. You can change the area A sitting inside the field — slide the loop into or out of the field region. Or you can change the orientation — spin the loop so that B⊥ shifts. That last trick can even flip the flux right around through the loop, and it’s exactly what every generator does — which is where the next page takes you.
ai.6: Back in 1831, Michael Faraday stumbled onto something marvellous: a magnetic flux that changes through a coil conjures up an emf — a voltage with enough push to drive a current all the way round a closed circuit. It shows up the moment the flux changes, by any of the three methods above, and it vanishes the instant the flux stops changing. Faraday’s law tells you how big that induced emf will be:
ai.7: Faraday’s law is all about rate. The induced emf cares about how fast the flux changes — that’s ΔΦB divided by Δt — and not one bit about how big the flux happens to be. A coil drenched in a huge but steady flux gives you zero emf; a coil whose tiny flux is whisked away in a millisecond can deliver a hefty one. Halve the time for the same flux change and you double the emf.
ai.8: Trap “An emf is induced because there’s flux through the coil” — nope. Flux on its own induces precisely nothing: a magnet sitting dead still inside a coil pours out plenty of flux and exactly zero emf. Only the rate of change of flux induces an emf, so every explanation you write must name the change.
ai.9: A coil of N turns acts like N loops wired in series. The very same changing flux threads through every single turn, and the per-turn emfs stack up — so double the turns and you double the emf. In the formula, ΔΦB is the flux change through just one turn; the factor N does the multiplying for you.
ai.10: Shanna is holding a 200-turn rectangular coil, 5.0 cm × 8.0 cm, with its plane perpendicular to a uniform 0.50 T magnetic field. She yanks the coil clean out of the field in 0.10 s.
ai.11: Formula: ε = N ΔΦBΔt (magnitude), where the flux through each turn is ΔΦB = B⊥A = 0.50 × (0.050 × 0.080) = 2.0 × 10⁻³ Wb.
ai.13: Answer: ε = 4.0 V — the average emf as the coil leaves the field. Pull it out in 0.050 s instead and you’d get 8.0 V: the identical flux change, just rushed through twice as fast.
ai.14: On a graph of flux against time, you read the induced emf straight off the gradient: ε = −N × gradient of the ΦB–t graph. A straight ramp means a steady emf; a steeper ramp means a bigger one; a turning point — where the flux momentarily peaks — means zero emf at that instant. And it runs both ways: the area under an ε–t graph equals NΔΦB, the total flux change times the number of turns. Figure 6.2 shows the pair of graphs for a magnet dropping through a coil: the emf slices through zero at the precise moment the flux peaks, and the exit pulse is taller and narrower than the entry pulse — because the accelerating magnet bolts out faster than it fell in — while the two pulse areas come out equal.
ai.15: Trap A ΦB–t graph and its ε–t graph are a gradient pair, never the same shape. The classic blunder is matching the look of one to the other — picking a flux graph shaped like the emf graph. Don’t. Read the gradient of the flux graph at each instant instead.
ai.16: Connects A changing magnetic field giving birth to an electric field is one half of the machinery behind an electromagnetic wave. In Unit 4, changing electric and magnetic fields keep regenerating each other and go racing through a vacuum as light.
ai.17: That negative sign in Faraday’s law is Lenz’s law in disguise: the induced emf drives a current whose own magnetic field fights the change in flux that created it. The opposition is always to the change, never to the field itself. While the flux through a loop is climbing, the induced current’s field points opposite to the applied field, battling the increase; while the flux is falling, the induced field swings round to point the same way as the applied field, propping it up.
ai.18: Trap “The induced field opposes the magnetic field” is wrong half the time. When the flux is dropping, the induced current’s field actually points the same way as the applied field — it’s opposing the decrease. Always state the change being opposed, never the field on its own.
ai.19: Lenz’s law is really conservation of energy in action. Imagine the induced current did the opposite and reinforced the change: the growing flux would induce a bigger emf, driving a bigger current, swelling the flux still further — and you’d be churning out electrical energy from nothing at all. Opposition is the only direction that keeps the energy books balanced, and it demands that work be done.
ai.20: Push the north pole of a magnet toward a coil and the induced current turns the near face of the coil into a north pole too — so the magnet gets shoved back, and the work you do fighting that repulsion is the very source of the electrical energy. It’s the same reason a generator grows stiffer to turn the more current it has to supply.
ai.21: To pin down the direction of an induced current, follow a three-step method:
ai.22: Try it on a north pole sailing straight toward a flat coil face: the flux through the coil points away from the magnet and is increasing; the induced field must point back toward the magnet to fight that increase; so with your thumb aimed at the magnet, your fingers curl out a current that runs anticlockwise as seen from the magnet. Pull the magnet away instead and the flux starts decreasing — and every direction flips.
ai.23: Key idea The induced emf drives a current whose effect opposes the change that produced it. Any other direction would conjure energy out of nothing, trampling conservation of energy.
ai.24: Connects A generator is just AOS 2’s DC motor run backwards — the very same coil-in-a-field hardware. Feed current in and the F = nIlB forces spin the coil; spin the coil and it pushes current out, and by Lenz’s law the magnetic force on that induced current drags against the rotation — so mechanical work in equals electrical energy out.
